22: A fulfillment: go and tell John what you have seen and heard — the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news proclaimed. Jesus's answer to the imprisoned John is the definitive self-identification from Isaiah: the signs that he lists are precisely the signs that Isaiah promised when YHWH comes to save (Isaiah 35:5-6, Luke 61:1). Christ's identity is proven not by Messianic title-claim but by Isaianic sign-fulfillment; the question is not what do you claim to be? but what do you see happening? The works are the revelation.
1: “When Jesus had finished saying all this to the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum” — the transition from the Sermon on the Plain to the healing of the centurion’s servant. The movement from teaching to healing enacts the Servant’s dual mission: to proclaim and to act. Capernaum as “his own city” (Matthew 9:1) is the base of the Galilean ministry — and the site where, by the end of the Gospel, Jesus will have done many of his works (Luke 10:15, the woe against Capernaum’s unbelief).
2: The centurion’s servant who is “dear to him” (entimos, highly valued/honored) is the first Gentile healing in Luke’s Gospel. The social situation — a Roman military officer seeking healing for a slave — crosses the boundaries of both ethnicity and social class. The centurion’s care for his servant demonstrates a humanity that transcends expected Roman treatment of slaves and anticipates the reversal of social hierarchies in the kingdom Christ proclaims.
3: The centurion sending Jewish elders as intermediaries demonstrates the honor-shame dynamic: he considers himself unworthy to approach Jesus directly (Luke 7:6). Yet the very indirection of his approach reflects the extraordinary faith Jesus will praise in Luke 7:9: the centurion believes that Jesus’s authority does not depend on his physical presence — a faith that exceeds Israel’s. The Jewish elders’ willingness to plead for a Gentile’s household reflects the unexpected coalitions the kingdom creates.
4: “This man deserves to have you do this” — the elders’ appeal to the centurion’s merit (axios) is ironically countered by the centurion’s own self-assessment (Luke 7:6: “I do not deserve to have you come under my roof”). The contrast between human declarations of merit and the centurion’s own humility is the Lukan pattern: those who declare themselves unworthy are the ones who receive; those who assert their worthiness are contrasted with those who receive grace. The centurion’s humility is itself the ground of Christ’s favor.
5: “He loves our nation and has built our synagogue” — the centurion’s love for Israel and his patronage of their worship anticipates the Gentile God-fearers of Acts (Cornelius, Acts 10:2: “he gave generously to those in need and prayed to God regularly”). The type: the Gentile who honors Israel’s God prefigures the Gentile mission in which non-Jews who feared God were the first recipients of the gospel. The centurion is a Cornelius-prototype, and his encounter with Jesus is a type of Acts 10.
6: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof” — the centurion’s self-assessment as unworthy is the model of the approach to Christ: not assertion of merit but acknowledgment of unworthiness. The Kyrie (Lord) address identifies Christ with divine authority — the centurion understands that he is addressing one whose presence in his house would constitute a kind of holy condescension, analogous to the divine presence descending to the Tabernacle (Exodus 40:34–35).
7: “But say the word, and my servant will be healed” — the centurion’s faith in the healing power of Christ’s word alone is the highest statement of Christological faith in the Galilean ministry. The word that heals is the divine creative word (Gen 1; Psalms 33:9: “he spoke, and it came to be; he commanded, and it stood firm”). The centurion recognizes in Jesus the one whose word carries divine authority across space — the sovereign word that creation itself obeys.
8: The centurion’s analogy from military authority to divine authority is the logic of the incarnation. A soldier obeys without knowing all the reasons; Jesus’s servant (creation, disease, death) obeys his word because of the authority behind it. The centurion has discerned something the disciples are still learning: Christ commands not by requesting but by the inherent authority of the one sent by the Father (John 5:19: “the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing”). His word carries the Father’s authority.
9: “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel” — the commendation of Gentile faith exceeding Jewish faith is both a Christological verdict and a prophetic sign. It fulfills Isaiah 49:6 (the Servant as “a light for the Gentiles”) and anticipates Luke’s two examples of Gentile/outsider favor in Jesus’s Nazareth sermon (Luke 4:25–27: the widow of Zarephath and Naaman the Syrian). The Gentile centurion’s faith is the first fruit of the universal harvest that Christ came to gather.
10: “The men who had been sent returned to the house and found the servant well (hygiainonta, in health)” — the healing in absentia accomplished by Jesus’s word is confirmed. The sign value is high: Jesus heals across distance by speech alone, demonstrating the omnipresent power of his word. The word-healing prefigures the cosmic scope of his lordship: in his resurrection and ascension, Christ’s word goes out to all nations (Matthew 28:18–20: “all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me”).
11: Nain (“beauty” in Hebrew) was a village in lower Galilee. Jesus arriving at the town gate just as a funeral procession is leaving is a providential encounter — Luke frames it as the Lord meeting death on its way out. The geographical movement (Jesus coming to the gate) mirrors the theological movement of the incarnation: the Author of life (Acts 3:15) enters the world to meet and reverse the procession of death that began in Gen 3 with Adam’s expulsion from the garden of life.
12: “The only son of his mother, and she was a widow” — the double vulnerability of the woman (widow + only son) intensifies the pathos and the Christological sign. The widow’s situation echoes the widow of Zarephath (1 Kings 17:17–24), whose only son Elijah raised — and Jesus cites this very story in Luke 4:25–26. Luke signals the Elijah typology: Jesus is the greater Elijah (the eschatological prophet like Moses, Deuteronomy 18:15), whose raising of the widow’s son exceeds even the Elijah prototype.
13: “When the Lord saw her, his heart went out to her (esplanchnisthē) and he said: Don’t cry” — divine compassion (splanchna, the Hebrew rahamim of God’s maternal mercy; cf. Isaiah 49:15) is the motivation of the miracle. Luke uniquely uses the title “the Lord” (ho Kyrios) for Jesus in narrative (not in dialogue) here — signaling that the compassion of the Lord of creation is personally present, acting on the widow’s behalf. The command “don’t cry” is eschatological: the Lord of life reverses what death produces.
14: “Young man, I say to you, get up!” (neaniske, soi legō, egerthēti) — the resurrection command. Egerthēti (the passive imperative of egeirō) is the resurrection verb used throughout the NT for Christ’s own raising (Luke 24:6; Acts 3:15; Romans 4:25). Touching the bier made Jesus ritually unclean (Numbers 19:11); his willingness to touch death to reverse it foreshadows the cross, where he enters fully into death to bring resurrection. The “I say to you” (not “in God’s name”) indicates self-authority, not prophetic agency.
15: “The dead man sat up and began to talk, and Jesus gave him back to his mother” — the precise echo of 1 Kings 17:23 (“Elijah picked up the child and carried him down from the room into the house. He gave him to his mother and said, ‘Look, your son is alive!’”). Luke’s deliberate echo establishes the Elijah typology while exceeding it: Elijah prayed three times and stretched himself over the boy; Jesus speaks a single word of command. The comparison highlights the eschatological upgrade: the greater prophet is here.
16: “A great prophet has appeared among us. God has come to help his people” — the crowd’s twofold response is both accurate and insufficient. “Great prophet” is true but partial (Elijah, Moses, one of the ancient prophets — cf. Luke 9:8, 19); “God has come to help his people” (epeskepsato, God has visited) is the deeper truth. Episkopē (divine visitation) is the language of Exodus 4:31 (Israel recognized God’s visitation in Moses) and Psalms 80:14 (“look down from heaven and see”). The crowd partially perceives: this is indeed the divine visitation, but in a person, not a prophet.
17: The news spreading throughout Judea and the surrounding country mirrors the spreading of the Servant’s fame in Isaiah 52:13–15 (“his appearance was so disfigured… but he will sprinkle many nations”). The report of Jesus’s works reaches to the regions that will be evangelized in Acts — the spreading news is the pre-evangelism that prepares the way. Every miracle-report is a seed of the resurrection proclamation: the one who raises the dead in Galilee will himself be raised in Jerusalem.
18: John’s disciples reporting to him “all these things” sets up the pivotal question of Luke 7:19. John in prison (Luke 3:20) is the type of the suffering precursor: Elijah (who prefigures John) suffered under Ahab and Jezebel, yet his work preceded the new order. John’s imprisonment does not stop the mission — his disciples continue as intermediaries between John and Jesus. The question John sends is the theological hinge of Luke 7: the imprisoned forerunner asking the Messiah to confirm his identity.
19: “Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?” — John’s question has exercised interpreters: is it doubt, or is John teaching his disciples to seek their answer from Jesus? Either way, the question is the right one, and Jesus’s answer is definitive. “The one who is to come” (ho erchomenos) is the technical messianic title — the coming one of Psalms 118:26, cited at the Triumphal Entry (Luke 19:38). John’s question frames the whole subsequent narrative as the answer to: is this the Messiah?
20: The repetition of John’s question (“Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?”) ensures the question is heard plainly by Jesus and by Luke’s reader. The directness — John sends his disciples to ask Jesus personally — mirrors the directness of the Christological question the whole Gospel asks. Luke frames Jesus’s entire ministry as the answer to this question: every healing, every teaching, every exorcism is evidence for the answer Jesus gives in Luke 7:22.
21: “At that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind” — the timing is crucial: Jesus answers John’s question not with a speech but with deeds. The healings at the moment of John’s question are themselves the answer, the living fulfillment of Isaiah’s signs. This is the epistemological method of the Gospels: Christ’s identity is verified by his works (John 5:36: “the works that the Father has given me to finish — the very works I am doing — testify that the Father has sent me”).
23: “Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me” — the beatitude after the Isaianic sign-list is a warning as well as a blessing. The “stumbling block” (skandalon) is the offense of the Christ who does not match expectations: he comes as a servant who heals and preaches rather than as a warrior-king who conquers. Isaiah 8:14 describes YHWH himself as “a stone that causes men to stumble and a rock that makes them fall.” Christ as the fulfillment of both the Servant and the divine Stumbling-Stone is the crux of the Gospel’s challenge.
24: “What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?” — Jesus’s eulogy of John begins by contrasting John with the unstable, the decorative, and the comfortable. The wilderness where John operated is the wilderness of Isaiah 40:3 (“a voice of one calling in the wilderness: Prepare the way for the LORD”). John in the wilderness is the herald who precedes the Lord’s advent — not a reed shaken by political winds but a fixed prophetic voice calling Israel to prepare for the visitation of YHWH.
25: “If not, what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? No, those who wear expensive clothes and indulge in luxury are in palaces” — John’s camel-hair clothing and locust diet (mentioned in Mark 1:6) are the marks of the prophetic counter-culture. The contrast with palatial luxury is also a contrast with Herod (in whose court John will be killed): the prophet who prepares the way for the Servant-Messiah wears the garb of the prophetic tradition (Zechariah 13:4), not the clothing of earthly kings.
26: “A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet” — Jesus’s confirmation that John is more than a prophet clarifies the prophetic category itself. The OT prophets pointed forward; John is the hinge — the last prophet who also functions as the forerunner at the moment of fulfillment. “More than a prophet” because he is the living transition point between promise and fulfillment, standing inside the stream of prophecy while pointing to the one in whom all prophecy is realized.
27: “This is the one about whom it is written: I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you” — a direct citation of Malachi 3:1 (with influence from Exodus 23:20), identifying John as the fulfillment of the Malachi messenger prophecy. The “you” addressed in Malachi 3:1 is YHWH himself (“who will prepare the way before me”), which Luke rephrases as “before you” — making Jesus the Lord YHWH whose way John prepares. The citation is one of Luke’s most direct identifications of Jesus with YHWH.
28: “I tell you, among those born of women there is no one greater than John; yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he” — the paradox reveals the eschatological divide. John is the greatest of the old order — the culminating prophet who stands on the threshold; but the kingdom inaugurated in Jesus surpasses the whole previous era. Even the least in the kingdom (who has received the Spirit, the new covenant heart, the forgiveness of sins in Christ’s name) stands on the far side of the divide that John could only approach. The kingdom is not a better version of the prophetic era; it is a new creation.
29: “All the people, even the tax collectors, when they heard Jesus’s words, acknowledged that God’s way was right, because they had been baptized by John” — John’s baptism of repentance prepared the people to receive Jesus’s proclamation. The tax collectors who received John’s baptism are the type of those who receive Christ’s grace: the marginalized, the sinners, those who know their need. Their acknowledgment of God’s justice (dikaioō ton theon) is the first step toward justification — the recognition that God is right and they are not.
30: “But the Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves, because they had not been baptized by John” — the irony is sharp: those who were the guardians of God’s purpose rejected it, while the despised tax collectors embraced it. The Pharisees’ refusal of John’s baptism is also a refusal of Christ, since John’s baptism was preparation for the coming one. The self-sufficiency that makes baptism unnecessary is the same self-sufficiency that makes the cross unnecessary — and thus excludes from its benefits.
31: “To what can I compare the people of this generation?” — the parable of the children in the marketplace addresses the generation that witnessed both John and Jesus and rejected both. The “this generation” language throughout Luke (Luke 7:31; Luke 9:41; Luke 11:29, 50–51; Luke 17:25) marks the generation that faces the decisive Christological choice: to receive or reject the Son of Man. The parable-form of the comparison is itself a Christological act: Jesus as the teacher of parables (Psalms 78:2) interprets the generation to itself.
32: “They are like children sitting in the market-place and calling out: We played the pipe for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not weep” — neither the joy of the gospel (John’s baptism of repentance leading to life) nor the mourning of judgment (the dirge) moved the generation. Christ as the one whose coming is the occasion for both joy (the Bridegroom’s arrival, Luke 5:34) and mourning (the call to repentance) is rejected by those who refuse both categories. The marketplace complaint reveals the generation’s fundamental uncharity.
33: “For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, He has a demon” — John’s asceticism — fasting, abstinence, wilderness discipline — is the Nazirite/Elijah pattern (Num 6; 1 Kgs 17–19). The accusation of demon-possession against ascetic fasting mirrors the accusation of demon-possession against Jesus’s exorcisms (Luke 11:15). Both John’s fasting and Jesus’s feasting are dismissed, demonstrating that the issue is not the form but the unwillingness to receive the message regardless of its packaging.
34: “The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and you say, Here is a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners” — Jesus’s self-description as “friend of tax collectors and sinners” is both an accusation he accepts and a revelation of his mission. “Friend” (philos) is what Abraham was called (Isaiah 41:8; James 2:23: “friend of God”). Christ as the friend of sinners is the one who crossed the social, moral, and religious boundary to share the table of those excluded from the covenant community, fulfilling Isaiah 53:12 (“he bore the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors”).
35: “But wisdom is proved right by all her children” — the final verdict: wisdom (Christ as incarnate Wisdom, 1 Corinthians 1:24,30; Colossians 2:3) is justified by those who receive her. The “children of wisdom” are those who respond rightly — the tax collectors who repented at John’s baptism and received Jesus’s welcome. Wisdom personified in Prov 8 cries out in the marketplaces; Jesus cried out in the marketplace (so to speak) through both John and himself; wisdom’s vindication is the community of those who received both.
36: The Pharisee’s dinner invitation and Jesus’s acceptance establishes the setting for the most dramatic public forgiveness scene in Luke’s Gospel. Jesus accepts the invitation (as elsewhere, Luke 19:5: “I must stay at your house today”) — his willingness to eat with Pharisees as well as tax collectors demonstrates the consistent pattern of table hospitality as the embodiment of kingdom welcome. The dinner table is the site of revelation: both the failure of Simon’s hospitality and the extravagance of the woman’s love will be displayed.
37: “A woman in that town who lived a sinful life learned that Jesus was eating at the Pharisee’s house, so she came there with an alabaster jar of perfume” — the woman’s identity as a public sinner and her act of bringing expensive perfume to anoint Jesus are types of the penitent’s approach to grace. The expensive perfume given without return echoes the Deuteronomic warning against half-hearted worship (Deuteronomy 15:10). Her costly gift is the act of one who has received the greater gift — forgiveness — and responds with extravagance that defies social calculation.
38: “She began to wet his feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair, kissed them and poured perfume on them” — a sequence of increasingly intimate acts of reverence: tears, drying with hair, kissing, anointing. Each act is a shadow of the ultimate anointing — the anointing of Jesus for burial that Mary of Bethany performs (John 12:3) before the crucifixion. The woman’s tears anticipate the mourning of the cross; her anointing prefigures the burial anointing. She performs unwittingly what the Gospel calls the disciples to understand: loving the one who goes to death for sinners.
39: “If this man were a prophet, he would know who is touching him and what kind of woman she is — that she is a sinner” — Simon’s reasoning fails on both counts: Jesus does know (Luke 7:40: “Jesus answered him” before Simon voiced the thought), and Simon’s assessment of the woman as merely a sinner is the assessment that the parable of the two debtors will overturn. The Pharisee’s “if this man were a prophet” is the exact opposite of the crowd’s recognition at Nain (Luke 7:16: “a great prophet has appeared among us”) — and both responses are surpassed by the truth.
40: “Simon, I have something to tell you” — the parable that follows is Jesus’s answer to Simon’s unspoken judgment. The willingness to address Simon’s private thought publicly demonstrates the same divine knowledge that Simon thought disqualified Jesus (Luke 7:39). Jesus speaks the parable to invite Simon into self-recognition — the same invitation that Nathan gave David with “you are the man” (2 Samuel 12:7). Christ as the parabolist who brings the heart’s hidden self-righteousness to light is doing what only the searcher of hearts can do (Ps 139; Jeremiah 17:10).
41: The parable of the two debtors — one owing five hundred denarii, one fifty — is a Christological parable about grace and gratitude. The economic framework (debt, forgiveness, love) mirrors the theological framework of the atonement: all have debts they cannot pay (Romans 3:23: “all have sinned”), and the forgiveness is unilateral. The two debtors represent the woman (large debt) and Simon (small debt in his own estimation) — and the parable’s reversal (the greater debt produces greater love) inverts the social hierarchy of the dinner party.
42: “Neither of them had the money to pay him back, so he forgave the debts of both” — the forgiveness is freely given to both, based entirely on the creditor’s decision, not on any merit of the debtors. The theme of unconditional forgiveness is grounded in the cross: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). The moneylender who forgives without payment is the shadow of the God who justifies the ungodly (Romans 4:5). The parable teaches that the objective reality of forgiveness precedes and produces the subjective experience of gratitude and love.
43: “I suppose the one who had the bigger debt forgiven” — Simon’s grudging answer (“I suppose,” hypolambanō) is a reluctant logic that his own estimate of himself will not follow. The comparison between the woman’s lavish love and Simon’s minimal hospitality will be made explicit in Luke 7:44–46. Simon is invited by his own answer to see himself as the debtor who loves little because he believes himself forgiven little — the parable’s judgment is absorbed through Simon’s own words.
44: “Do you see this woman? I came into your house. You did not give me any water for my feet, but she wet my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair” — the contrast between Simon’s failure of basic hospitality and the woman’s extravagance exposes Simon’s heart. In the honor-shame culture, failing to provide water for the guest’s feet was a notable slight. The woman’s tears replacing the water is an act of self-humiliation that surpasses all social obligation. Christ who received such extravagant devotion will himself wash his disciples’ feet (John 13:5) — the Lord enacting the servant’s role.
45: “You did not give me a kiss, but this woman, from the time I entered, has not stopped kissing my feet” — the kiss of greeting was standard hospitality (cf. Romans 16:16: “greet one another with a holy kiss”); its absence from Simon signals cool formality rather than warm welcome. The woman’s continuous kissing of his feet is the posture of a penitent before a king — the devotion of one who knows she stands before grace itself. The contrast continues to expose the heart: formal observance vs. love born of received forgiveness.
46: “You did not put oil on my head, but she has poured perfume on my feet” — anointing the head of a guest with oil was a mark of honor (Psalms 23:5: “you anoint my head with oil”; Psalms 133:2). Simon’s omission is the third mark of minimal hospitality. The woman’s pouring expensive perfume on his feet is the extravagant reversal — she gives what is costlier (perfume vs. oil) in the more humble place (feet vs. head). The inversion encodes the kingdom’s logic: the one who humbles herself before Christ is the one who most honors him.
47: “Therefore, I tell you, her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown. But whoever has been forgiven little loves little” — the declaration of forgiveness is retroactive: the woman’s love demonstrates prior forgiveness, not that her love caused it. This is the crucial distinction: forgiveness precedes love as the tree precedes the fruit. The parable has already established this (Luke 7:42: “he forgave the debts of both”). The Christological core: Jesus speaks as the one who has the authority to forgive sins — which the guests immediately question in Luke 7:49.
48: “Then Jesus said to her: Your sins are forgiven” — the direct declarative forgiveness. This is the same formula used in the paralytic’s healing (Luke 5:20: “your sins are forgiven”), which triggered the scribes’ accusation of blasphemy (Luke 5:21). The authority Jesus exercises here is the authority that belongs to God alone (Isaiah 43:25: “I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions, for my own sake, and remembers your sins no more”). The woman’s sins are forgiven by the one who is the incarnation of the divine forgiver.
49: “Who is this who even forgives sins?” — the table guests’ question is the central Christological question of the passage (and of the whole Gospel). Their question implies the correct theological premise (only God forgives sins, Psalms 103:3; Isaiah 43:25) while failing to draw the correct Christological conclusion (therefore, this man is God in human form). The question “who is this?” echoes the storm-disciples’ question (Luke 8:25: “who is this? He commands even the winds and the water”) — the Gospel invites its readers to answer what the characters cannot.
50: “Your faith has saved you (sesōken); go in peace” — the salvation-word and the shalom-blessing close the passage with the double gift: salvation (comprehensive deliverance from sin and its consequences) and peace (the shalom of restored relationship with God, Isaiah 57:19: “peace to those far and near”). The faith that saves is trust in the person of Jesus — the woman did not articulate a theology; she acted in love toward the one who had forgiven her. The peace Christ pronounces is the peace that the cross purchases (Colossians 1:20: “making peace through his blood, shed on the cross”).
